title: Vectors as Locations and Directions
learningObjectives: Understand vectors as both points in space and geometric translations
Perform vector addition and scalar multiplication
Recognize that each dimension of an activation vector does not necessarily have a fixed meaning

whyThisLesson.text: Before we can understand how neural networks represent concepts, we must establish a rigorous foundation in vector arithmetic. In deep learning, every concept is ultimately embedded in high-dimensional vector spaces. A strong intuitive grasp of vectors as both locations in space and directions of movement allows us to manipulate semantic meanings, understand algebraic relationships, and lay the groundwork for later interpretability tasks like SetConCA.

sections.bridgeFrom: Introduction to the structure of the curriculum

intuition.analogy: Imagine a vector as a GPS coordinate on a map. A vector [3,4] can either mean 'go to the point at x=3, y=4', or it can mean the instruction 'travel 3 miles east and 4 miles north'. Both representations are valid and computationally identical.
intuition.analogyLimit: This analogy breaks down because spatial GPS maps have fixed cardinal directions (North, South) that mean the same thing everywhere. In a neural network's latent space, the axes themselves often have no fixed, interpretable meaning.
intuition.text: In a neural network, an activation vector is an array of numbers. Geometrically, this array specifies coordinates in a multi-dimensional space. By adding vectors, we translate one point along the direction of another. This means semantic relationships can be mapped to spatial translations. If we add a 'direction' vector to a 'location' vector, we effectively move through concept space.

toyExample.description: We will compute vector additions and observe how vectors translate space.

steps.insight: The new point is shifted 1 unit right and 2 units up from a. Shows geometric translation.

visual.question: How does vector addition look visually?
visual.description: A 2D coordinate plane showing vectors a, b, and their sum a+b forming a parallelogram.

technical.text: Mathematically, vectors in Rn are ordered lists of n real numbers. Vector addition is performed element-wise, meaning the i-th component of the sum is the sum of the i-th components of the operands. Scalar multiplication scales a vector's magnitude without altering its underlying directional line, simply multiplying each component by the scalar. These operations are the foundation of linear combinations.

definitions.definition: An array of numbers representing a point or direction in a vector space.

mathematics.label: Vector notation
mathematics.plainExplanation: A vector is a list of components.
mathematics.whyNeeded: To formalize the mathematical object we are operating on.
mathematics.encourages: Treating activations as structured algebraic objects.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That any individual component is meaningful.

mathematics.label: Vector addition
mathematics.whyNeeded: To combine representations or apply translations.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the resulting vector remains in the manifold of valid representations.

mathematics.label: Scalar multiplication
mathematics.plainExplanation: Multiply every element of a vector by a number.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That meaning is preserved at extreme scales.

evidence.claim: Word2Vec 'King-Man+Woman=Queen' as application of vector arithmetic
evidence.label: [Word2Vec application]
evidence.paraphrase: Early word embedding models demonstrated that vector arithmetic corresponds to analogical reasoning.

failureModes.name: Basis aligned assumption
failureModes.mechanism: Assuming each dimension of an activation vector has a fixed meaning.
failureModes.symptom: Failing to find meaning when looking at single neurons.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check if concepts are directions rather than axis-aligned.
failureModes.mitigation: Look for directions in the full vector space.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Activation vectors are the inputs to SAEs and SetConCA.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Activation vectors are the inputs to SAEs and SetConCA.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Why we need to transform activations.

exercises.prompt: If a=[3,4] and b=[1,2], what is a+b?

masteryQuestions.prompt: Why is it risky to interpret the meaning of a single dimension in a dense activation vector?
rubric: Must mention arbitrary rotation of basis.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Because neural networks typically do not align concepts with canonical axes.

bridgeToNext.text: Now that we know how to move in vector spaces, we measure distance.
